coredata.dc:title: Phase diagram of the Sn–Zn–Ti system
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Vassilev, Gueorgui P.
Dobrev, Evgeni S.
Tedenac, Jean-Claude
coredata.dc:description: Equilibrated Sn–Zn–Ti alloys and (Sn+Zn)/α-Ti diffusion couples have been studied by scanning electron microscopy, metallography, and differential scanning calorimetry. For the first time an isothermal section, at 600°C, of the ternary Sn–Zn–Ti system has been constructed. A previously unknown ternary phase with approximate formula Ti3Sn2Zn6 (probable homogeneity interval in the range Ti5Sn4Zn11 to Ti5Sn3Zn12) has been found. The solubility ranges of the titanium based solid solutions and the intermetallic phases have been looked for. As far as we could detect and in agreement with theoretical considerations, zinc dissolves more in Ti–Sn phases than tin into Ti–Zn compounds. Titanium additions of 3 and 4at.% Ti do not influence significantly the Sn–Zn eutectic temperature. The experimentally determined melting enthalpies of the nearly eutectic alloys have values around 100Jg−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ternary alloy systems
Phase diagrams

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental information about the binary end-systems (Ti–Zn, Sn–Zn, Ti–Sn)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Spectrum, in characteristic X-rays, of the ternary Sn–Zn–Ti compound with approximate formula Ti3Sn2Zn6 (scanning electron microscope point analysis of specimen no. 3). (B) (unpublished) Spectrum of Si alloyed Ti5Sn3 phase (8.5at.% Si) in characteristic X-rays. The energy of the radiation (keV) is plotted along the abscissa. The counted intensity (number of impulses per 100s) is plotted along the ordinate.

Micrograph of specimen no. 3, in back scattered electrons. The former liquid solution (1) is represented by tin-rich light areas, with nearly eutectic zinc content, and zinc dendrites (3). The solid phase (point 2) (well shaped hexagonal crystals) corresponds approximately to the formula Ti3Sn2Zn6 (Table 3). The compound Ti6Sn5 (4) is confined in Ti3Sn2Zn6 crystals.

(A) Isothermal section of the phase diagram Sn–Zn–Ti at 600°C. The compositions of the phases are determined by microprobe analyses and are denoted by means of the following symbols: (o) Ti2Sn3; (♢) Ti6Sn5; (
), Ti3Sn2Zn6; (●) Ti3Sn; (■) α-Ti; (▴), TiZn5; (♦) TiZn3; (▾) L (liquid solutions). The thick solid lines symbolize the phase boundaries, while the thin solid lines indicate hypothetical tie-lines in the two-phase fields. The dotted lines symbolize the most uncertain phase boundaries. (B) The chemical compositions of the equilibrated specimens and the initial compositions of the liquid end-members are represented as follows: (▴) chemical compositions of specimens nos. 1–5, 7, 8; (▾) initial compositions of specimens 6, 9 and 10. The following symbols notify the diffusion paths: the thickest solid lines (when the diffusion paths pass through monophase regions or along the tie-lines in the two phase regions); dotted lines (when the diffusion paths pass through three-phase regions or cuts the tie-lines in the two-phase regions).

Differential scanning calorimetry curves of specimen nos. 1–3 (Table 5) at heating. The numbers of the curves correspond to the specimens’ numbers. Heating rate: 10°min−1. The temperature (°C) is plotted along the abscissa and the heat flow (mW)—along the ordinate.
Description of the binary phases relevant to the Ti–Sn–Zn system [17,21,26,38]

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: P.s., Pearson symbol; s.g., space group; S.d., Strukturbericht designation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Both formulae are assumed appertaining to one phase (TiZn16).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: May be identical with TiZn8 or with TiZn7.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical composition of the first series of specimens

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: No, consecutive number; X Ti, X Sn, X Zn, mole fractions of the respective elements; n.a., not appropriate.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summarized experimental data for the specimens annealed at 600°C (phases and chemical compositions identified by microprobe analyses)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para:
X
i , mole fraction of the pertinent element.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diffusion paths, revealed by scanning electron microscopy, in diffusion couples (specimens nos. 6, 9 and 10), at 600°C (873K)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: No, number of the specimen; L X Zn (Zn mole fractions), initial chemical composition of the liquid phase, constituted by zinc and tin.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: //Symbolizes discontinuity of the diffusion path (i.e. the experimentally observed phase fields, located on the both sides of the sign // are not neighbors in the phase diagram).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical compositions (atomic percents, at.%) of the specimens, used for differential thermal analyses

article.ce:title: Phase diagram of the Sn–Zn–Ti system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Sofia, Faculty of Chemistry, 1J. Bourchier Av., 1164 Sofia, Bulgaria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Montpellier II, LPMC, 34095 Montpellier, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Equilibrated Sn–Zn–Ti alloys and (Sn+Zn)/α-Ti diffusion couples have been studied by scanning electron microscopy, metallography, and differential scanning calorimetry. For the first time an isothermal section, at 600°C, of the ternary Sn–Zn–Ti system has been constructed. A previously unknown ternary phase with approximate formula Ti3Sn2Zn6 (probable homogeneity interval in the range Ti5Sn4Zn11 to Ti5Sn3Zn12) has been found.

The solubility ranges of the titanium based solid solutions and the intermetallic phases have been looked for. As far as we could detect and in agreement with theoretical considerations, zinc dissolves more in Ti–Sn phases than tin into Ti–Zn compounds. Titanium additions of 3 and 4at.% Ti do not influence significantly the Sn–Zn eutectic temperature. The experimentally determined melting enthalpies of the nearly eutectic alloys have values around 100Jg−1.

ce:section.ce:para: Tin and zinc are both among the prospective constituents of the multicomponent lead-free solders [1]. Moreover, evidences exist that alloying with small quantities of titanium ameliorate the wetting and mechanical properties of some materials [2–5].

From the other side, the interactions of high-melting (in this case, titanium) and low-melting (zinc, tin, bismuth, etc.) elements represent permanent interest in the prospect of joining materials by means of the transition liquid phase (TLP) method.

A tentative thermodynamic optimization of the binary end-systems Ti–Zn and Ti–Sn as well as of the Sn–Zn–Ti system has been done by Ono et al. [6] in order to obtain thermodynamic functions needed for assessment of the surface tension of the ternary liquid phase. Nevertheless, those authors ignored the existence of some binary phases (e.g. Ti2Sn3, TiZn7) as well as the ternary compound with approximate composition Ti3Sn2Zn6 reported below.
The purpose of the present work is to investigate experimentally the Sn–Zn–Ti phase diagram, mainly by the construction of an isothermal section at 600°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental information about the binary end-systems (Ti–Zn, Sn–Zn, Ti–Sn)
ce:section.ce:para: Numerous authors have studied the system Ti–Zn [7–17]. Recently, Vassilev et al. [18] constructed a new version of the zinc-rich side of this phase diagram (Table 1 ).

The Sn–Zn phase diagram is relatively well investigated [19–26]. A number of thermodynamic optimizations of this system are available as well [27–29]. This interest is due to the importance of these alloys for the development of lead-free solders [30].

The Ti–Sn alloys have been subject of many investigations as well [31–37]. Even so, Kuper et al. [38] have recently revealed a formerly unknown stable phase (Ti2Sn3) whose crystal structure (Table 1) has been identified by Künnen et al. [39] later.

The syntheses of the ternary alloys have been done in quartz tubes sealed under vacuum of around 1Pa. Precisely weighed (±0.0001g) fine pieces of purified by chemical gas-transport reaction Ti (5N), pellets of Sn (4N) and Zn (4N) have been mixed. The tubes of the first series of experiments (nos. 1–4, Table 2 ) have been gradually heated up during 72h to 800°C, kept at this temperature for 24h, annealed at 600°C during 21 days, and quenched in water. The specimens of the second series (nos. 4–10) have been annealed for 24h at 800°C and furthermore 2 months at 600°C.

Fifteen ampoules have been produced, but ten alloys have been prepared and studied successfully, only. The reason is that in a number of experiments, titanium did not react with the other constituents, probably, due to the formation of oxide layers during the sealing or afterwards. In several damaged ampoules dark films formed along the tubes’ walls.

Other potential experimental challenges are due to some thermodynamic reasons. Namely, the large negative standard Gibbs energies of formation of titanium oxides [40,41] allow to the pure titanium to react with SiO2 (the walls of the tubes). Nevertheless, we have already encountered and overcome this difficulty in a series of works involving the synthesis of titanium containing alloys in quartz tubes [18,42–45]. During those studies we have found evidences that a reaction between the tube walls and the titanium could start at temperatures higher than around 820°C only. For this reason, the upper limit of the annealing temperature applied in the present work was set to be 800°C.

Scanning electron microscopy, optical microscopy and differential scanning calorimetry have been applied in this work. Analyses for Si, Ti, Sn and Zn have been done in order to find out the chemical compositions of the phases and to control an eventual contamination.

The identified phases and their chemical compositions are shown in Table 3 . An important result, for the construction of the Sn–Zn–Ti phase diagram, is the discovery of a formerly unknown ternary phase.

In Fig. 1 A is exhibited a spectrum of this compound in characteristic X-rays. For the sake of comparison (with a case when Si is present) in Fig. 1B (unpublished) the spectrum of a Ti5Sn3 phase (containing 8.5at.% Si) is shown. The latter result has been obtained during authors’ study of the Ti–Sn–Si system [46]. As one can see, the Kα-line of Si is situated at around 1.8keV, i.e. it would be seen in Fig. 1A, if silicon was present in that specimen.

Micrograph of specimen no. 3, in back scattered electrons, is shown in Fig. 2 . The specimen has been cooled fast by immerging in cold water, thus zinc dendrites have appeared, while the tin-rich light areas have nearly eutectic zinc content. The hexagonal crystals belong to the newly found ternary phase (Table 3).

The statistical treatment of the results obtained for the chemical compositions of that phase leads to the following mean values and their standard deviations (for a 95% confidence interval): X Ti =0.25±0.01; X Sn =0.19±0.02; X Zn =0.56±0.02. In general, the stoichiometric numbers corresponding to such a composition region would correspond to the formulae Ti5Sn4Zn11–Ti5Sn3Zn12. The experimental data indicate the possibility for existence of a homogeneity range. For the sake of brevity, however, we shall use in this work a tentative and simplified formula for the ternary phase, namely, Ti3Sn2Zn6.

The liquid end-members of the diffusion couples (specimen nos. 6, 9 and 10, see Tables 2 and 4 ) are tin+zinc melts, while the single solid phase, present since the start of the annealing, is the hexagonal modification of the titanium (α-Ti). The notions characterizing the formation of the reactionary diffusion layers aught to be valid in these cases. Contemporary conceptions [47,48] concerning the relations between the diffusion phenomena and the corresponding ternary phase diagram have been already used by the present authors to the investigations of the Ti–Bi–Zn [42] and Ti–Bi–Sn [45] systems. Similar approach is applied to the present work too.

As shown in Table 4, the diffusion paths in diffusion couples nos. 6 and 10 start from the tin-rich side and pass through titanium-tin compounds. In specimen no. 6, adjacent Ti6Sn5 and TiZn3 layers have been observed. This finding has been taken into account for the construction of the isothermal sections (Fig. 3 A and B).

Some compounds, expected in the diffusion zones have not been registered. This phenomenon is typical for the growth of intermediate layers. Thus, the phases found in the present investigations will be mentioned explicitly: liquid solutions, Ti3Sn2Zn6, Ti2Sn3, Ti6Sn5, Ti3Sn, α-Ti, TiZn3 and TiZn5. The compounds that have not been observed are represented in Fig. 3A and B according to pertinent literature data [18,26,38].

Recent authors’ studies of the binary Ti–Zn system [18] have shown that a contentious phase (TiZn5 situated between TiZn7 and TiZn3), possibly, does not exactly correspond to the supposed formula. The melting temperature of this phase is around 615°C, thus above the working temperature of 600°C. The richest in zinc compound, registered in the present work, has a ratio between zinc and titanium mole fractions amounting to 5.28 (Table 3, spec. 9). That is the reason to suppose that it could be identified as TiZn5.

The measured zinc solubility in Ti6Sn5 and Ti2Sn3 is 7 and 2at.%, respectively. The tin content in TiZn3 is up to around 2at.% (Table 3). These experimental observations are in agreement with theoretical considerations based on the values of Pauling electro-negativities (1.54, 1.65 and 1.96, for Ti, Zn and Sn, in that order [49]) and empirical atomic radii (135, 142 and 145pM, for Ti, Zn and Sn, respectively).

Differential thermal analyses (DTA) have been performed with three specimens, equilibrated at 600°C, by differential scanning calorimeter TA 2960 Instruments. The compositions of the specimens are in the vicinity of the eutectic point of the binary Sn–Zn system (Table 5 ).

The main purpose of these experiments was to find out the influence of small titanium additions to the binary Sn–Zn eutectic temperature (198.5°C) according to Massalski [26]. One of the alloys (Table 5, no. 1) is binary Sn–Zn specimen. The DTA of this specimen supplies an internal standard, usable for comparison. As one can see, in Fig. 4 , a lowering of the eutectic melting temperature could not be observed (in the frame of the experimental uncertainties).

The experimentally determined melting enthalpies of these nearly eutectic alloys vary from 98 to 101Jg−1. These values are in relatively fair agreement with the respective enthalpy change (76Jg−1), calculated using the optimized parameters of Fries and Lukas [28].

The existence of a formerly unknown ternary compound with approximate formula Ti3Sn2Zn6 (possible homogeneity interval in the range Ti5Sn4Zn11–Ti5Sn3Zn12) has been found for the first time. The crystals of this phase exhibit hexagonal shape and compositions in the following range: X Ti =0.25±0.01; X Sn =0.19±0.02; X Zn =0.56±0.02. Experimental data about the zinc solubility in Ti2Sn3 (around 2at.% Zn) and Ti6Sn5 (around 7at.% Zn) as well as about the tin solubillty in TiZn3 (around 2at.% Sn) have been obtained.
Isothermal section, at 600°C, of the ternary phase diagram Sn–Ti–Zn has been constructed using data of equilibrated alloys and diffusion couples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A Ph.D. bourse attributed by the French Government to Mr. E. Dobrev and short contracts of Dr. G.P. Vassilev with CNRS-France are gratefully acknowledged.
This work is related to the European action for development of lead-free solders COST 531.

Parts of these studies have been presented to the following scientific meetings as posters: CALPHAD XXXII, 25–30 May 2003, Quebec, Canada; XXX JEEP (Journeys of Phase Equilibria Studies), 30 March–1 April 2004, St. Avold, France; CALPHAD XXXIII, 30 May–4 June 2004, Krakow, Poland.
